original_abstract:
We introduce Quadratic Span Programs (QSPs), a new characterization of the NP complexity class that naturally extends the span programs originally defined by Karchmer and Wigderson. Our primary objective is the efficient construction of succinct, non-interactive arguments that can be verified with minimal computational overhead. To achieve this, QSPs utilize a novel approach to the arithmetization of Boolean circuits, leading to significant performance gains over traditional methods. 

By leveraging QSPs, we construct a Non-Interactive Zero-Knowledge (NIZK) argument for Circuit-SAT in the Common Reference String (CRS) model. Our construction consists of only seven group elements, while the CRS size and prover computation time remain quasi-linear. These theoretical improvements translate into highly practical results; our implementation demonstrates that this NIZK argument achieves the shortest proofs and the most efficient prover and verifier performance reported to date. 

Furthermore, we present Quadratic Arithmetic Programs (QAPs), a variation of QSPs designed to naturally handle arithmetic circuits over large fields. We demonstrate how both QSPs and QAPs can be applied to the public verification of outsourced computation. In this setting, a client delegates the computation of a function to a server and must verify the result in significantly less time than it would take to perform the calculation locally. Our resulting schemes represent the most efficient general-purpose protocols for publicly verifiable computation currently available.
humanized_abstract:
We introduce the concept of Quadratic Span Programs (QSPs), a new characterization of NP via span programs, a concept introduced by Karchmer and Wigderson. We aim to design polynomial time algorithms to generate succinct, proof codes that yield very efficient verification. We use a new arithmetization of Boolean circuits that enables us to achieve a considerable improvement over known alternatives.

Using QSPs we prove the existence of a Non-Interactive Zero-Knowledge (NIZK) argument for Circuit-SAT in the Common Reference String (CRS) model. Our construction consists of only seven group elements and the CRS size and the prover runtime are quasi-linear. In particular, we show that the NIZK argument is in practice as fast as one can hope for: Our implementation provides the shortest proofs and fastest prover and verifier times up to the knowledge of our report.

In addition, we show how Quadratic Arithmetic Programs (QAPs) can be used to deal with computations involving arithmetic circuits over large fields. Finally, we show how to use both QSPs and QAPs to publicly verify arbitrary computations given by an outsourced server. The public verification of outsourced computation is the client passing computations to be performed to a server, and wishing to verify the result almost as quickly as it could perform the computation locally. Our solutions are the most efficient general-purpose publicly verifiable computation protocol so far.
